---
title: "Wooldridge Introductory Econometrics -- Ch. 5: Multiple regression: OLS asymptotics (via StatEcon)"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

**Source.** Stata examples reproduced from Wooldridge, *Introductory Econometrics: A Modern Approach* (Cengage). Data are the book's companion files.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)
```

Residuals aligned to the rows of the original data frame (Stata's `predict r, resid`).

```{julia}
predict_res(m, df, y) = df[!, y] .- FixedEffectModels.predict(m, df)
```

## Standard Errors in a Birth Weight Equation

Stata runs the same log birth-weight equation on the first 694 observations and
then on the full sample of 1,388, illustrating how standard errors shrink as
*n* grows (OLS asymptotics).

```{julia}
#=
use "data/bwght.dta", clear
reg lbwght cigs lfaminc in 1/694

      Source |       SS           df       MS      Number of obs   =       694
-------------+----------------------------------   F(2, 691)       =     10.52
       Model |   .80921458         2   .40460729   Prob > F        =    0.0000
    Residual |  26.5787136       691   .03846413   R-squared       =    0.0295
-------------+----------------------------------   Adj R-squared   =    0.0267
       Total |  27.3879282       693   .03952082   Root MSE        =    .19612

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      lbwght | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
        cigs |  -.0046368   .0013319    -3.48   0.001    -.0072519   -.0020216
     lfaminc |   .0194044   .0081884     2.37   0.018     .0033273    .0354815
       _cons |   4.705583    .027053   173.94   0.000     4.652467    4.758699
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
bwght = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("bwght"))
ols_694 = stata_regress(bwght[1:694, :]; y = :lbwght, x = [:cigs, :lfaminc])
```

```{julia}
#=
reg lbwght cigs lfaminc

      Source |       SS           df       MS      Number of obs   =     1,388
-------------+----------------------------------   F(2, 1385)      =     18.31
       Model |  1.29879229         2  .649396147   Prob > F        =    0.0000
    Residual |  49.1215413     1,385  .035466817   R-squared       =    0.0258
-------------+----------------------------------   Adj R-squared   =    0.0244
       Total |  50.4203336     1,387  .036352079   Root MSE        =    .18833

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      lbwght | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
        cigs |  -.0040816   .0008582    -4.76   0.000    -.0057651    -.002398
     lfaminc |   .0162657   .0055833     2.91   0.004      .005313    .0272183
       _cons |   4.718594   .0182445   258.63   0.000     4.682804    4.754384
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
ols_full = stata_regress(bwght; y = :lbwght, x = [:cigs, :lfaminc])
```

Both fits reproduce the published coefficients, standard errors, *t*-statistics
and confidence limits to the printed digits (`cigs = -.0046368` at *n*=694,
`-.0040816` at *n*=1388). The full-sample SE on `cigs` (`.0008582`) is roughly
55% of the subsample SE (`.0013319`), consistent with the `1/√n` shrinkage.

## Example 5.3: Economic Model of Crime

```{julia}
#=
use "data/crime1.dta", clear
reg narr86 pcnv ptime86 qemp86

      Source |       SS           df       MS      Number of obs   =     2,725
-------------+----------------------------------   F(3, 2721)      =     39.10
       Model |  83.0741941         3   27.691398   Prob > F        =    0.0000
    Residual |  1927.27296     2,721  .708295833   R-squared       =    0.0413
-------------+----------------------------------   Adj R-squared   =    0.0403
       Total |  2010.34716     2,724  .738012906   Root MSE        =     .8416

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      narr86 | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
        pcnv |  -.1499274   .0408653    -3.67   0.000    -.2300576   -.0697973
     ptime86 |  -.0344199    .008591    -4.01   0.000    -.0512655   -.0175744
      qemp86 |   -.104113   .0103877   -10.02   0.000    -.1244816   -.0837445
       _cons |   .7117715   .0330066    21.56   0.000      .647051     .776492
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
crime1 = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("crime1"))
crime53 = stata_regress(crime1; y = :narr86, x = [:pcnv, :ptime86, :qemp86])
```

The auxiliary regression regresses the OLS residual on the original regressors
plus two excluded variables (`avgsen`, `tottime`). Because the residual is
orthogonal to the fitted regressors, the coefficients on `pcnv`, `ptime86`,
`qemp86` collapse to ~0; the *F*-test on the added regressors is the LM idea.

```{julia}
#=
predict ubar, resid
reg ubar pcnv ptime86 qemp86 avgsen tottime

      Source |       SS           df       MS      Number of obs   =     2,725
-------------+----------------------------------   F(5, 2719)      =      0.81
       Model |  2.87904835         5  .575809669   Prob > F        =    0.5398
    Residual |  1924.39392     2,719  .707757969   R-squared       =    0.0015
-------------+----------------------------------   Adj R-squared   =   -0.0003
       Total |  1927.27297     2,724  .707515773   Root MSE        =    .84128

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        ubar | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
        pcnv |  -.0012971    .040855    -0.03   0.975    -.0814072    .0788129
     ptime86 |  -.0048386   .0089166    -0.54   0.587    -.0223226    .0126454
      qemp86 |   .0010221   .0103972     0.10   0.922    -.0193652    .0214093
      avgsen |  -.0070487   .0124122    -0.57   0.570     -.031387    .0172897
     tottime |   .0120953   .0095768     1.26   0.207    -.0066833     .030874
       _cons |  -.0057108   .0331524    -0.17   0.863    -.0707173    .0592956
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
crime1.ubar = predict_res(crime53, crime1, :narr86)
aux53 = stata_regress(crime1; y = :ubar,
                       x = [:pcnv, :ptime86, :qemp86, :avgsen, :tottime])
```

The Julia residual regression reproduces Stata exactly: coefficients on the
originally included regressors are ~0 (`pcnv = -.0012971`), the two added
variables are jointly insignificant (`F(5, 2719) = 0.81`, `Prob > F = 0.5398`),
and `R² = 0.0015`. This matches the published table to the printed digits.
